items.prompt_md: In the monotonic-stack solution to Daily Temperatures, what do we keep on the stack?
options: The temperatures themselves, sorted from coldest to warmest
Indices of days that are still waiting for a warmer day
Indices of days that have already found their warmer day
A running maximum temperature seen so far
items.answer: Indices of days that are still waiting for a warmer day
items.explanation_md: The stack holds the indices whose answer isn't known yet. The moment a warmer day arrives, we pop and resolve every index it beats — that's why we store indices (to compute `i - j`), not temperatures.

items.prompt_md: While scanning left to right, the current temperature is warmer than the temperature at the index on top of the stack. What happens?
options: Pop that index and record the answer, then keep checking the new top the same way
Push the current index and leave the stack top alone
Discard the current temperature and move on
Swap the current index with the top of the stack
items.answer: Pop that index and record the answer, then keep checking the new top the same way
items.explanation_md: A single warmer day can resolve several waiting days in a row, so popping continues in a `while` loop, not just once, until the top is no longer beaten.

items.prompt_md: After all the popping is done for the current index, what happens to that index?
options: It gets pushed onto the stack, since it still needs its own warmer day
It is discarded, since it already found an answer
It is pushed twice, once for itself and once for the day it resolved
Nothing — indices are never pushed after the first day
items.answer: It gets pushed onto the stack, since it still needs its own warmer day
items.explanation_md: Every index goes on the stack exactly once when it arrives; resolving other indices doesn't exempt it from needing to wait for its own warmer day.

items.prompt_md: At the very end of the scan, some indices are still sitting on the stack. What does that mean?
options: Those days never found a warmer day for the rest of the array, so their answer stays 0
Those days are the warmest in the array
The algorithm has a bug and must rescan
Those indices should be popped and answered with the array length
items.answer: Those days never found a warmer day for the rest of the array, so their answer stays 0
items.explanation_md: The answer array is initialized to all zeros; any index that never gets popped simply keeps that default, meaning no warmer day ever showed up.

items.prompt_md: Why does the stack need to store indices rather than the temperatures themselves?
options: Because the final answer is a distance (`i - j`), which requires knowing the position, not just the value
Because indices take less memory than temperatures
Because temperatures cannot be compared directly
It doesn't matter — either works identically
items.answer: Because the final answer is a distance (`i - j`), which requires knowing the position, not just the value
items.explanation_md: The value can always be looked up as `temps[j]` when needed for comparison, but the distance between two days can only be computed if the position `j` was kept.

items.prompt_md: What invariant does the monotonic stack maintain at every point during the scan?
options: The temperatures at the stacked indices, read from bottom to top, are in decreasing order
The stacked indices are always sorted numerically
The stack always contains exactly one element
The stack alternates between warm and cold days
items.answer: The temperatures at the stacked indices, read from bottom to top, are in decreasing order
items.explanation_md: Anything that would break the decreasing order gets popped and resolved immediately, so what's left on the stack is always consistent with that order.

items.prompt_md: A buggy version pushes every index but never pops anything. What goes wrong?
options: Every day's answer stays 0, since a day is only resolved by being popped when a warmer day appears
The program crashes immediately
It still produces the correct answer, just slower
It only fails on strictly increasing temperature sequences
items.answer: Every day's answer stays 0, since a day is only resolved by being popped when a warmer day appears
items.explanation_md: The pop step is exactly where an answer gets written; skipping it means the answer array never gets updated, no matter what shows up later.

items.prompt_md: How would the invariant change if the question asked for the next *smaller* temperature instead of the next greater one?
options: The comparison flips: pop while the new value is *smaller* than the top, keeping the stack increasing instead of decreasing
Nothing changes — the same code works unmodified
The stack would need to store temperatures instead of indices
A monotonic stack could no longer be used at all
items.answer: The comparison flips: pop while the new value is *smaller* than the top, keeping the stack increasing instead of decreasing
items.explanation_md: The whole trick is symmetric: 'pop while I violate your order' just needs the inequality reversed to track increasing order and resolve 'next smaller' instead.

items.prompt_md: On the input `[73, 74, 75, 71, 69, 72]`, why does index 3 (temperature 71) get popped by index 5 (temperature 72) but not by index 4 (temperature 69)?
options: 69 is colder than 71, so it doesn't beat it, while 72 is warmer than 71 and resolves it
Index 4 is skipped by the algorithm entirely
Popping only happens on even indices
71 and 69 are never compared because they're adjacent
items.answer: 69 is colder than 71, so it doesn't beat it, while 72 is warmer than 71 and resolves it
items.explanation_md: A day only pops the top of the stack when it's strictly warmer than it; a colder day just gets pushed on top and waits its own turn.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of the monotonic-stack solution to Daily Temperatures?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
items.answer: O(n)
items.explanation_md: Every index is pushed exactly once and popped at most once across the entire run, so the total work is O(n) time and O(n) worst-case space, even though a single day can trigger a burst of pops.

items.prompt_md: What is the worst-case space complexity of the monotonic stack, and on which kind of input does it occur?
options: O(n), on a strictly decreasing sequence of temperatures where nothing ever gets popped
O(1), because the stack never grows past a constant size
O(log n), since only the largest values stay on the stack
O(n^2), because every index can be pushed multiple times
items.answer: O(n), on a strictly decreasing sequence of temperatures where nothing ever gets popped
items.explanation_md: If every day is colder than the one before it, no pops ever happen, so every index stays on the stack and its size grows linearly with the input.

items.prompt_md: A naive fix rescans forward from every index looking for the first warmer day. What is its worst-case time complexity, and on what input does it hit that worst case?
options: O(n^2), on a strictly decreasing sequence where each rescan runs almost to the end of the array
O(n log n), regardless of the input
O(n), same as the monotonic stack
O(1), since rescans are cheap in practice
items.answer: O(n^2), on a strictly decreasing sequence where each rescan runs almost to the end of the array
items.explanation_md: On a strictly decreasing run, no index ever finds a warmer day nearby, so its rescan has to go almost all the way to the end — repeated for every index, that's O(n^2).

items.prompt_md: Even though a single index popped during one iteration can resolve several waiting indices at once, why does the total work across the whole array still stay O(n) rather than O(n^2)?
options: Each index can only ever be pushed once and popped once, so the total number of pushes plus pops across the whole run is bounded by 2n
Because the array is always sorted before the scan begins
Because pops are free and don't count toward the complexity
Because the stack is capped at a fixed maximum size
items.answer: Each index can only ever be pushed once and popped once, so the total number of pushes plus pops across the whole run is bounded by 2n
items.explanation_md: Amortized analysis: a burst of several pops in one iteration is 'paid for' by those indices' one-time push earlier, so summed over the whole array the work is still linear.

items.prompt_md: For every element in an array you need the distance to the next element that is strictly greater than it, and a nested-loop rescan from each index would be O(n^2). Which approach fits?
options: Sort the values, then binary-search for each element's neighbor
Slide a window and shrink it from the left whenever a condition breaks
Keep a stack of indices still waiting for a bigger value, popping and resolving them as bigger values arrive
Build a hash map from value to its first occurrence
items.answer: Keep a stack of indices still waiting for a bigger value, popping and resolving them as bigger values arrive
items.explanation_md: "Next greater element, single left-to-right pass" is the monotonic-stack fingerprint: each index is pushed once and popped at most once, so the whole array costs O(n) even though any one day can resolve several waiting days at once.

items.prompt_md: Given an array of heights, for each building you need to know how many buildings to its right you can see before one at least as tall blocks the view — effectively the nearest element that is greater than or equal to the current one.
options: Push heights (or indices) onto a stack, popping whenever the incoming height is at least as tall as the top
Sort the heights and binary search for each one
Use a hash map counting how many times each height appears
Use two pointers starting from opposite ends of the array
items.answer: Push heights (or indices) onto a stack, popping whenever the incoming height is at least as tall as the top
items.explanation_md: 'Nearest blocking element to the right' is the next-greater-element signal in disguise — a monotonic stack resolves every building the instant something tall enough shows up.

items.prompt_md: You need, for each day in a list of stock prices, the number of consecutive days before it (including itself) where the price has been less than or equal to today's price — the 'stock span' problem.
options: Keep a monotonic stack of (price, span) pairs, popping while the top's price is less than or equal to today's
Sort all prices and binary search for today's rank
Use a hash map from price to its most recent index
Use a sliding window that shrinks whenever price drops
items.answer: Keep a monotonic stack of (price, span) pairs, popping while the top's price is less than or equal to today's
items.explanation_md: Each day's span depends on how far back prices stayed lower — a monotonic stack collapses all the resolved lower days in one pop burst, same idea as next-greater-element.

items.prompt_md: Given a histogram of bar heights, you need the area of the largest rectangle that fits entirely under the bars, which requires knowing, for each bar, the nearest shorter bar on each side.
options: Use a monotonic increasing stack of bar indices to find the nearest shorter bar on each side in one pass
Sort the bars by height and greedily pick the tallest ones
Use a hash map counting how many bars share each height
Use two pointers narrowing in from both ends of the histogram
items.answer: Use a monotonic increasing stack of bar indices to find the nearest shorter bar on each side in one pass
items.explanation_md: 'Nearest shorter bar on each side' is exactly the next-smaller-element question, which a monotonic stack answers for every bar in a single O(n) pass.

items.prompt_md: For each car in a line of cars (given position and speed), you need to know the nearest car ahead of it that it will eventually catch up to, in order to group cars into collision 'fleets'.
options: Process cars from front to back with a monotonic stack, popping any car whose arrival time is beaten by the one behind it
Sort the cars by speed and pair adjacent ones
Use a hash map from position to speed
Use a sliding window over consecutive car pairs
items.answer: Process cars from front to back with a monotonic stack, popping any car whose arrival time is beaten by the one behind it
items.explanation_md: 'Which car ahead does this one eventually merge into' is a nearest-boundary question resolved in a single directional pass — the monotonic stack signal.

items.prompt_md: You need to remove the fewest possible digits from a number's string representation so the remaining digits form the smallest possible number, keeping the original order.
options: Keep a monotonic increasing stack of digits, popping a bigger digit off the top whenever a smaller one arrives and removals remain
Sort the digits and drop the largest ones
Use a hash map to count digit frequencies
Use two pointers from both ends of the string
items.answer: Keep a monotonic increasing stack of digits, popping a bigger digit off the top whenever a smaller one arrives and removals remain
items.explanation_md: A smaller digit 'beats' a larger digit sitting just before it, and a monotonic stack greedily pops that larger digit the instant a smaller replacement shows up — same resolve-on-arrival shape as next-greater-element.

items.prompt_md: You're given an array of integers and simply need to know, for each value, how many times it occurs in total, with order and position irrelevant.
options: Tally counts in a hash map keyed by value
Keep a monotonic stack of values, popping whenever a duplicate arrives
Push every value and pop when the count exceeds one
Use a monotonic stack of indices to track occurrences
items.answer: Tally counts in a hash map keyed by value
items.explanation_md: There's no 'next greater/smaller' or ordering relationship being asked about here — it's a pure frequency count, which a hash map answers directly with no stack needed.

items.prompt_md: Given two arrays, you need to find every value that appears in both, regardless of position or order.
options: Put one array's values in a hash set, then check membership while scanning the other
Push one array onto a stack and pop while scanning the other
Keep a monotonic stack to track the smallest shared value
Use a stack to detect out-of-order matches between the arrays
items.answer: Put one array's values in a hash set, then check membership while scanning the other
items.explanation_md: This is a set-membership question with no 'next greater/smaller' relationship or nesting order, so a hash set is the right tool, not a monotonic stack.

items.prompt_md: Given an array of integers, you need to know whether any two numbers sum to a given target value.
options: Hash map of value to index, checking for the complement as you scan
Monotonic stack of values, popping whenever two values sum to the target
Push values onto a stack and pop until the top plus the current value equals the target
Keep a stack of the smallest values seen so far
items.answer: Hash map of value to index, checking for the complement as you scan
items.explanation_md: Two-sum asks 'has the complement been seen', a direct lookup question with no ordering or nesting relationship — a hash map, not a stack, is the tool.

items.prompt_md: You need to group a list of words so that every anagram of the same letters ends up in the same group.
options: Hash map keyed by each word's sorted letters (or letter counts), collecting words that share a key
Push each word onto a stack and pop whenever an anagram is found
Keep a monotonic stack ordered by word length
Use a stack to compare adjacent words in the list
items.answer: Hash map keyed by each word's sorted letters (or letter counts), collecting words that share a key
items.explanation_md: Grouping by a derived key (sorted letters) is a hashing question — there's no 'next greater/smaller' or nested resolution being asked for here.

items.prompt_md: For each element in an array, you need to find the nearest element to its left that is strictly smaller than it, and the array is large enough that a nested-loop rescan would be too slow.
options: Keep a monotonic increasing stack of values, popping while the top is greater than or equal to the current value
Tally counts in a hash map and compare
Sort the array and binary search for each element
Use two pointers scanning from both ends inward
items.answer: Keep a monotonic increasing stack of values, popping while the top is greater than or equal to the current value
items.explanation_md: 'Nearest smaller element, brute force too slow' is the mirror image of next-greater-element — same monotonic-stack mechanism, just with the comparison and direction flipped.
